title: Strong Eigenstate Thermalization from Mean-Ergodic Non-chaotic Dynamics
authors: Avadhut V. Purohit, Harshit Sharma, Udaysinh T. Bhosale
abstract: We report an example of a many-body system, derived from the double kicked top (DKT), with non-chaotic yet mean-ergodic dynamics that displays \textit{strong} eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) in the quantum regime. The analysis addresses a key open question: whether \textit{strong} ETH is a quantum analog of ergodicity (or mean-ergodicity). Despite non-chaotic dynamics, the fluctuations of the diagonal matrix elements of an observable scale as $D^{-1/2}$, where $D$ denotes the Hilbert space dimension. Furthermore, the off-diagonal matrix elements show Gaussian statistics together with a smooth function $f_O(\bar{E}, ω)$ that becomes nearly uniform in the large-$k_θ$ domain. Our findings show that even mean-ergodic and non-chaotic systems can exhibit \textit{strong} ETH.
summary: This paper shows that strong thermalization (strong ETH) can occur in quantum systems even when the underlying classical dynamics are non-chaotic, provided they are mean-ergodic. By studying the Double Kicked Top model, the authors prove that ergodicity alone, rather than chaos, is sufficient to drive the emergence of Gaussian matrix element statistics and $D^{-1/2}$ scaling.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the strong Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) can emerge from classical non-chaotic but mean-ergodic dynamics, addressing whether strong ETH is a quantum analog of ergodicity rather than specifically a consequence of chaos.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a non-chaotic, mean-ergodic regime of the Double Kicked Top model exhibits strong ETH, characterized by Gaussian statistics in off-diagonal matrix elements and $D^{-1/2}$ scaling of diagonal fluctuations.
method: The study employs classical analysis (phase-space portraits, KSE, and ergodicity testing) alongside quantum analysis (ETH ansatz testing, spectral form factor, and higher-order spacing ratio statistics).
model_or_system: A many-body system derived from the Double Kicked Top (DKT) model, consisting of $N=2j$ spin-1/2 particles with all-to-all interactions, characterized by kick strengths $k_r$ and $k_{	heta}$.
key_observables: Diagonal and off-diagonal matrix elements of the observable $J_z^2 / [j(j+1)]$, Kolmogorov-Sinai entropy (KSE), von Neumann entropy, and spacing ratio statistics $r(	ilde{k})$.
important_parameters: Kick strengths $k_r$ (radial) and $k_{	heta}$ (angular); regimes include non-chaotic/non-ergodic, non-chaotic/mean-ergodic, and chaotic.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the Floquet operator formalism and the applicability of von Neumann's mean-ergodic theorem to the classical limit.
figures_summary: Figures include phase-space portraits, plots of expectation values and fluctuations, distributions of matrix elements, and spectral diagnostics like the Spectral Form Factor and spacing ratio distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ETH in non-chaotic systems, describes the DKT model, presents classical ergodicity and chaos analysis, moves to quantum ETH verification via matrix element statistics, and concludes with spectral and correlation-based proofs of integrability/chaos.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it identifies a mechanism for 'chaos-free thermalization,' which could potentially serve as a resource for quantum computing by generating entanglement without the destructive effects of quantum chaos.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Blue dots indicate a period-3 cycle in the left panel. The right panel shows non-chaotic, mean-ergodic evolution of the same initial point illustrated with black dots.
